title: FOIA Request Guide 2026 | Five Steps to Get Federal Records
subtitle: The Freedom of Information Act gives any person the right to request federal agency records. This step-by-step protocol covers agency targeting, the precision scoping framework, fee waiver eligibility, and the 20-day enforcement clock.
thumbnail_alt: Open government documents and federal records folders on a desk

content_html:
The Freedom of Information Act, codified at 5 U.S.C. § 552, gives any person, including non-citizens and non-journalists, the right to request records from executive branch federal agencies. FOIA does not cover Congress, the courts, or the White House Office. It covers every federal department, bureau, commission, and agency from the EPA to the FAA to the Department of Defense.

The process requires precision. Vague requests are legally deniable as unreasonably burdensome. Requests sent to the wrong agency component disappear into administrative limbo. This protocol is built around the five procedural chokepoints where requests fail, and how to clear each one.

Step 1 | Identify the Correct Component

Large federal departments are divided into components, each with its own FOIA office. The Department of Justice, for example, has 29 separate FOIA offices covering components from the FBI to the Bureau of Prisons to the Antitrust Division. A request sent to the DOJ's main FOIA intake will be forwarded, but the forwarding process adds weeks and the receiving component may dispute jurisdiction.

The correct approach is to identify the specific agency or sub-component that generated, received, or maintains the records you want before submitting. For commercial drone telemetry, that is the FAA's Operations Support Group, not the Department of Transportation's main FOIA office. For clinical trial correspondence, that is FDA's specific Center directorate, not the broader Department of Health and Human Services.

The FOIA.gov agency directory lists every federal FOIA office with direct contact information and component breakdowns. Use it before writing the request, not after.

Step 2 | Use Centralized Digital Portals

As of 2026, the majority of federal agencies process FOIA requests through digital submission portals rather than email or postal mail. FOIA.gov provides a unified intake interface that routes requests to participating agencies and provides a centralized tracking dashboard. Submitting through FOIA.gov creates an immediately timestamped record of submission, which matters for enforcing the 20-day response clock covered in Step 5.

Several high-volume agencies maintain their own portals outside the FOIA.gov ecosystem. The Department of Defense uses the PAL (FOIA Portal for All Lookups) system. The CIA, NSA, and other intelligence community components have separate classified and unclassified intake portals. For these agencies, use the agency-specific portal rather than FOIA.gov, which may not route to the correct intelligence community component.

If an agency does not yet have a digital portal, submit by certified mail with return receipt. The certified mail receipt date is your legal submission timestamp.

Step 3 | Draft with Hyper-Specific Scope

The single most common reason FOIA requests are denied or returned without processing is overbreadth. The phrase "any and all documents regarding" a broad topic is legally vulnerable to an administrative denial as "unreasonably burdensome" under 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(3)(A), which requires that requests "reasonably describe" the records sought.

A well-scoped request uses four concrete parameters. First, a defined date range: "all internal memoranda dated between January 1, 2025, and March 31, 2026" is processable. "All documents about the project" is not. Second, specific document types: "internal memoranda, email correspondence, and meeting minutes" rather than "documents." Third, specific identifying terms: project code names, contract numbers, specific software system names, or named individuals involved in the matter. Fourth, a requested format: asking for records in their original digital format, whether CSV, JSON, or native PDF, reduces the agency's ability to downgrade data to scanned paper copies that eliminate machine-readable information.

The precision framework applies to the subject line and the request body. Agencies use the subject line to route requests to the correct office. A subject line reading "FOIA Request, Contract DTFAWA-23-R-00123, Period January 2024 to December 2025" routes faster and more accurately than "FOIA Request for drone records."

Step 4 | Claim Your Fee Waiver

Agencies charge search fees, duplication fees, and in some cases review fees for processing FOIA requests. Search fees typically run at the salary rate of the personnel conducting the search, which can reach several hundred dollars per hour for complex requests requiring senior analyst review.

Congress built a fee waiver provision into FOIA specifically for requesters who intend to disseminate the information publicly and whose request is not motivated by commercial gain. Under 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(4)(A)(iii), a fee waiver must be granted if disclosure is "in the public interest because it is likely to contribute significantly to public understanding of the operations or activities of the government" and the disclosure is "not primarily in the commercial interest of the requester."

Journalists, bloggers, researchers, and documentary makers all qualify. The fee waiver language must be included in the request itself. The standard formulation is: "I request a waiver of all fees associated with this request. The requested records are not sought for commercial use. I am a journalist publishing information for public consumption. Disclosure of these records will contribute significantly to public understanding of federal agency operations."

If a fee waiver is denied, you have the right to appeal the denial before paying any fees. This is a separate, parallel appeals process from the substantive record request.

Step 5 | Enforce the 20-Day Clock

5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(6)(A)(i) requires agencies to determine within 20 working days whether to comply with a FOIA request and to notify the requester of that determination. The clock begins on the day the request is received by the correct FOIA office, not the day you submitted it. This distinction matters: requests sent to a wrong component may not start the clock until they are forwarded.

In practice, most agencies issue an automated extension notice citing "unusual circumstances" under 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(6)(B). Unusual circumstances extensions are capped at 10 additional working days. Beyond that, agencies must place requesters on a first-in, first-out processing queue and notify them of their position.

If an agency fails to issue any determination within the statutory window, the requester is deemed to have constructively exhausted administrative remedies and may file suit in federal district court without waiting for an administrative appeal. In practice, filing a formal administrative appeal, which requires a response within 20 additional working days from a senior supervisor, is faster and produces records in the majority of cases without litigation.

Track every submission with its tracking number, submission date, and 20-day deadline in a dedicated log. FOIA.gov provides automated status updates for requests submitted through its portal. For agency-specific portals, check status manually every five business days after the initial 20-day window passes.

Key Resources

The primary federal FOIA portal is FOIA.gov, maintained by the Department of Justice Office of Information Policy. The Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press maintains the Open Government Guide, which covers state-level records laws in addition to federal FOIA. MuckRock, a nonprofit journalism platform, provides FOIA filing tools and publishes request outcomes that can be used as precedent for scoping similar requests.

For OzoneNews investigative coverage using public records, see the News desk. For related federal agency coverage including FAA aerospace filings and NASA program documents, see NASA program coverage and Tech policy reporting.

Frequently Asked Questions

Who can file a FOIA request?
Any person, including non-US citizens and non-journalists, can file a FOIA request for federal executive branch agency records. There is no credential or residency requirement. FOIA does not apply to Congress, the federal courts, or the White House Office.

How long does a FOIA response take?
The statutory deadline is 20 working days. In practice, complex requests to high-volume agencies such as the FBI or DHS take months to years due to administrative backlogs. Simple requests for records that have been frequently requested before can be fulfilled in days using an agency's reading room of previously released documents.

What happens if an agency misses the 20-day deadline?
The requester is deemed to have constructively exhausted administrative remedies and may file a formal administrative appeal or file suit in federal district court. Filing an administrative appeal is generally faster and triggers a 20-day senior supervisor review cycle.
